mission.title: Install and Configure Hyperlight on Kubernetes
mission.description: Hyperlight is a lightweight Virtual Machine Manager (VMM) designed for embedding within applications, enabling the safe execution of untrusted code in micro virtual machines with minimal overhead. Installing Hyperlight allows developers to leverage its capabilities for creating secure and efficient environments for running untrusted code.

steps.title: Create a Namespace for Hyperlight
steps.description: First, create a dedicated namespace for Hyperlight to isolate its resources.

steps.title: Apply the Hyperlight Deployment
steps.description: Next, apply the Hyperlight deployment manifest to your cluster.

steps.title: Apply the Hyperlight Service
steps.description: Then, apply the Hyperlight service manifest to expose the application.

steps.title: Verify the Installation
steps.description: Finally, verify that the Hyperlight pods are running successfully.

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Hyperlight will show the pods in the 'hyperlight' namespace as running when you execute `kubectl get pods --namespace hyperlight`. You will also be able to access the service via port 8080 on your local machine.

uninstall.title: Remove Hyperlight Resources
uninstall.description: To uninstall Hyperlight, delete the deployment and service, and then the namespace.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Hyperlight
upgrade.description: To upgrade Hyperlight, fetch the new version's manifests and reapply them.

troubleshooting.title: Pod Not Starting
troubleshooting.description: Check the pod logs for errors using: `kubectl logs <pod-name> -n hyperlight`.

troubleshooting.title: Service Not Accessible
troubleshooting.description: Ensure that the service is correctly configured and check its endpoints with: `kubectl get endpoints -n hyperlight`.

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient Resources
troubleshooting.description: If pods are in a pending state, verify that your cluster has sufficient resources available.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have kubectl installed and configured to communicate with your Kubernetes cluster.
